items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.
It's Steele and Aronson. “Tajfel and Turner” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The individualism-collectivism cultural dimension was identified by:
items.why: It's Hofstede. “Asch” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Tajfel and Turner publish social identity theory?
items.why: The real figure is 1979 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
